item.title: New Energy Management Experts in Varese: Excellence and Competence
item.description: The Order of Industrial Experts of Varese certifies new EGE, energy management experts, ready to lead the energy transition.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- New EGE certified in Varese
- High-profile training course
- Experts in integrated energy management

## Key facts
- **What**: Certification of new EGEs
- When: March-April 2026
- Where: Varese, Italy
- Who: Order of Industrial Engineers of the Province of Varese
- Amount: Not yet specified

Between March and April 2026, the Order of Industrial Engineers of the Province of Varese, through its association Apiv, completed a high-profile training course. This course culminated in the qualifying exam for the prestigious role of EGE, Energy Management Expert. The EGE is not just a technician but a highly specialized professional: a certified figure capable of managing energy complexity through an integrated vision. These professionals combine deep technical and environmental expertise with a solid mastery of managerial and financial processes.

The path to certification was anything but simple. Candidates had to meet the stringent and rigorous requirements imposed by the certification body, which analyzed with extreme rigor the combination of academic qualifications and years of field experience. Only those who demonstrated excellence in the energy sector were able to access the final tests, which were brilliantly and successfully passed.

### Implications for the Energy Sector

The certification of new EGEs represents a significant step for the energy sector, especially in an increasingly ambitious European and national regulatory context. These new experts will be fundamental for the requalification of buildings and companies, actively contributing to the future energy transition. Their integrated competence will allow addressing energy challenges with a holistic vision, combining technical, environmental, and financial aspects.

The president of the Order, Roberto Banfi, and the president of the Association, Sergio Dozio, expressed their highest praise and heartfelt congratulations to their colleagues for the extraordinary achievement. They emphasized that this result is not only a success for the category of Industrial Engineers but a true pride for the entire province of Varese.

### The Role of EGEs in the Energy Transition

EGEs will be the true protagonists of the future energy transition. Their ability to manage energy complexity through an integrated vision makes them indispensable in a continuously evolving landscape. Their expertise will be crucial to address the challenges posed by increasingly stringent European and national regulations, oriented towards sustainability.

In a context where energy management has become a priority, the figure of the EGE represents a reference point for companies and entities that want to optimize their energy consumption and reduce environmental impact. Their experience and competence will be fundamental to guide the sector towards a more sustainable and efficient future. ## Analysis of EGE Skills

Certified EGEs in Varese possess advanced technical and environmental skills, combined with a solid understanding of managerial and financial processes. This combination of skills makes them unique in the professional landscape, capable of tackling energy challenges with an integrated and holistic vision.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before the introduction of the EGE role, energy management was often entrusted to specialists focused on individual aspects of the sector. The lack of an integrated vision made it difficult to effectively address energy challenges. With the advent of EGEs, companies and organizations can rely on professionals capable of managing energy complexity in a comprehensive and coordinated manner.

### Future Scenarios

In an increasingly sustainability-oriented future, EGEs will be fundamental in driving the energy transition. Their expertise will be crucial in addressing the challenges posed by increasingly stringent European and national regulations, aimed at reducing emissions and improving energy efficiency.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers in the energy sector, EGE certification represents an opportunity for professional growth. The possibility of collaborating with these certified experts can offer new career prospects and enhance skills in the energy sector.

### Procedures to Obtain EGE Certification

To become an EGE, it is necessary to follow a rigorous training path and pass an enabling exam. The path includes the acquisition of advanced technical and environmental skills, combined with a solid understanding of managerial and financial processes. The certification is issued by a recognized certification body, which rigorously evaluates the academic qualifications and field experience of candidates.

### Useful Tools for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers interested in obtaining EGE certification, it is possible to consult the website of the Ordine dei Periti Industriali Laureati della Provincia di Varese for detailed information on the training path and the required prerequisites. Additionally, tools such as the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can be used to evaluate career opportunities in the energy sector.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## Concrete Actions for Cross-Border Workers

For cross-border workers who wish to embark on the path to becoming an EGE, it is important to follow some concrete steps. First, it is necessary to verify the access requirements for the training path, which include academic qualifications and years of field experience. Subsequently, you can enroll in the training path offered by the Order of Chartered Industrial Experts of the Province of Varese.

### Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Check Requirements**: Verify the access requirements for the training path, which include academic qualifications and years of field experience.
2. Enrollment in the Training Path: Enroll in the training path offered by the Order of Chartered Industrial Experts of the Province of Varese.
3. Completion of the Path: Follow the training path and pass the qualifying exam to obtain the EGE certification.
4. Use of Skills: Apply the skills acquired in the energy sector, contributing to the energy transition of the future.

### Deadlines and Required Documents

To complete the training path and obtain the EGE certification, it is necessary to respect the deadlines set by the Order of Chartered Industrial Experts of the Province of Varese. The required documents include academic qualifications, field experience certificates, and passing the qualifying exam.

### Useful Tools and Resources

For cross-border workers interested in obtaining the EGE certification, tools such as the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can be used to evaluate career opportunities in the energy sector. Additionally, you can consult the website of the Order of Chartered Industrial Experts of the Province of Varese for detailed information on the training path and the necessary requirements.

### Conclusion

The EGE certification represents an opportunity for professional growth for cross-border workers in the energy sector. By following the concrete steps and using the available tools, it is possible to obtain the EGE certification and actively contribute to the energy transition of the future.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/eccellenza-e-competenze-anche-a-varese-i-nuovi-esperti-in-gestione-dellenergia-dellordine-dei-periti-industriali-laureati/2559424/)*

item.title: Half of Switzerland sees the system as unfair
item.description: A survey reveals that over half of the Swiss population considers the social system unfair, with an increase in dissatisfaction compared to 2024.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Over half of the Swiss population perceives the system as unjust
- A third finds it 'somewhat unjust', 18% 'completely unjust'
- Income is the decisive factor

## Key facts
- **What**: Survey on the perception of social justice
- When: April 2026
- Where: Switzerland
- Who: Beobachter magazine and gfs.bern institute
- Amount: 2,000 people surveyed

Over half of the Swiss population perceives the system as unjust, according to a representative survey published Monday by Beobachter magazine, conducted by the gfs.bern research institute on around 2,000 people. Compared to the first 'Justice Barometer' of summer 2024, dissatisfaction has grown: a third of those surveyed consider the system 'somewhat unjust', while 18% judge it 'completely unjust'. The decisive factor is income: those with little income feel systematically disadvantaged. While two-thirds of the wealthy declare themselves satisfied, three-quarters of low-income individuals express a negative judgment.

The percentage of those who feel treated 'rather' or 'very' justly has decreased from 68% to 56% compared to 2024. 'In the end, it all depends on who benefits from the system and who bears the burden,' says pollster Cloé Jans. According to the researcher, the issue of costs dominates every other question, starting with rents and health insurance premiums. On an individual level, the wallet is under pressure, on a political level, there are disputes over billion-dollar projects, for example also in the field of pensions.

Among the various topics addressed, interesting is the one concerning the legal field: 81% of those surveyed say they are concerned about the high costs of legal proceedings and lawyers, which are considered too high. The feeling is growing that justice in Switzerland has become purchasable. Despite everything, the survey also signals a positive note: trust in the pillars of the State – democracy, freedom and education – remains solid.

### Implications for cross-border workers

For cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy, this perception of social injustice can have various implications. For example, economic pressure can influence the decision to move to Switzerland permanently or to continue working cross-border. Furthermore, concern about legal costs can make it more difficult to access justice for those residing in another country.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Compared to 2024, dissatisfaction has increased significantly. This could be due to various factors, including the rise in the cost of living, tax pressure, and general economic difficulties. For cross-border workers, this means they may have to face additional challenges in managing their finances and planning for the future.

### What to do if injustice is perceived

If injustice is perceived in the system, it is important to act. First of all, it is essential to inform oneself about one's rights and the possibilities of legal assistance. Furthermore, participating in public discussions and supporting organizations that work for social justice can make a difference. For cross-border workers, it is also useful to stay updated on regulations and assistance opportunities available both in Switzerland and in Italy. ## Practical Analysis

The increasing dissatisfaction with social equity in Switzerland has several practical implications, especially for cross-border workers in Ticino. The perception of injustice can influence the decision to continue working cross-border or to move to Switzerland permanently. Additionally, concerns about legal expenses can make it more difficult for those residing in another country to access justice.

### Tax Implications

For cross-border workers, economic pressure can influence the decision to move to Switzerland permanently or to continue working cross-border. For example, tax pressure can be a decisive factor. In Switzerland, the withholding tax system can simplify tax returns for cross-border workers, but it can also result in higher tax pressure compared to Italy. It is important to inform oneself about tax regulations and the assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy.

### Access to Justice

Concerns about legal and attorney fees can make it more difficult for cross-border workers to access justice. It is important to inform oneself about one's rights and the possibilities for legal assistance. Additionally, participating in public discussions and supporting organizations that work for social justice can make a difference. For cross-border workers, it is also useful to stay updated on the regulations and assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy.

### Financial Planning

Economic pressure can influence the financial planning of cross-border workers. It is important to consider legal expenses and the opportunities for assistance available. Additionally, it is useful to inform oneself about tax regulations and the assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy. Financial planning can help manage one's finances and plan for the future more effectively.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Action

If you perceive injustice in the system, it's important to act. First and foremost, it's crucial to inform yourself about your rights and the possibilities for legal assistance. Additionally, participating in public discussions and supporting organizations that work for social justice can make a difference. For cross-border workers, it's also useful to stay updated on regulations and assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy.

### Concrete steps

1. **Inform yourself about your rights**: Knowing your rights is the first step to addressing the perception of injustice. You can consult the regulations and assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy.
2. Participate in public discussions: Participating in public discussions can help you make your voice heard and support organizations that work for social justice.
3. Stay updated: Staying updated on regulations and assistance opportunities available can make a difference in managing your finances and planning for the future.
4. Use assistance tools: Using assistance tools available in both Switzerland and Italy can help you manage your finances and plan for the future more effectively.

### Useful tools

For cross-border workers in Ticino, it's useful to use assistance tools available in both Switzerland and Italy. For example, the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can help you manage your finances and plan for the future more effectively. Additionally, you can consult the regulations and assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy.

### Conclusion

The increase in dissatisfaction with social equity in Switzerland has several practical implications, especially for cross-border workers in Ticino. It's important to inform yourself about your rights and the possibilities for legal assistance, participate in public discussions, and support organizations that work for social justice. Additionally, it's useful to stay updated on regulations and assistance opportunities available in both Switzerland and Italy. Using available assistance tools can help you manage your finances and plan for the future more effectively.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/oltre-la-met%c3%a0-della-popolazione-percepisce-il-sistema-come-ingiusto/91322586)*

item.title: Retired workers in Switzerland: +220,000 by 2025
item.description: By 2025, 220,000 people will continue working past the age of 65. A growing phenomenon, with 4.5% of employees over 64.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- 220,000 workers over 65 in Switzerland by 2025
- +133,000 in 20 years
- 4.5% of employees are at least 65 years old

## Key facts
- **What**: Number of workers of retirement age
- When: 2005 vs 2025
- Where: Switzerland
- Who: Federal Statistical Office (FSO)
- Amount: 220,000 in 2025 vs 87,000 in 2005

In 2025, around 220,000 people continued to work after reaching the standard retirement age in Switzerland. This figure, which represents a significant increase from the 87,000 recorded in 2005, emerged from an analysis by the news agency AWP of data from the Swiss Labour Force Survey (SLFS) by the Federal Statistical Office (FSO). The share of workers aged 64 and over among all employed people has risen from 2.1% to 4.5%. In other words, today one in twenty workers is at least 65 years old.

### Demographic transformation
The trend reflects the demographic transformation of the country. The so-called baby boomers, born during a period of strong population growth, are entering the post-retirement age group, making the over-64 age group numerically larger. However, this is not just a demographic phenomenon: more and more people are choosing to remain active in the labour market even after formal retirement. Currently, 12% of people aged 65 and over are in employment, double the 7% recorded in 2005.

### Changes in employment forms
The phenomenon affects men and women equally. At the same time, forms of employment are changing: among those who continue to work, more than half are self-employed and the vast majority work part-time. This indicates increasing flexibility in the Swiss labour market, which allows many people to combine work with retirement.

### Impact on the Canton of Ticino
In the Canton of Ticino, this trend has a significant impact, especially for cross-border workers who may consider continuing to work beyond retirement age. The proximity to Italy and the job opportunities offered by Switzerland make Ticino an attractive place for those who wish to extend their professional careers. Moreover, the presence of an established community of cross-border workers facilitates integration and continuity of employment for those who decide to remain active beyond the age of 65.

### Practical implications
For cross-border workers in Switzerland, continuing to work after the age of 65 can have important implications. For example, it is necessary to consider the impact on pensions, such as AHV and BVG, as well as on health insurance such as LAMal. In addition, it is essential to be aware of tax regulations, particularly with regard to withholding tax and tax returns. The choice to continue working can significantly influence the financial and pension situation, making careful planning essential.

To delve deeper into the tax and pension implications, you can use the [tax calculator](nav:tax-return) to estimate the taxes to be paid and the [pension calculator](nav:pension) to assess the impact on your pension. Additionally, it is useful to consult the specific guides for cross-border workers available on the website to better understand the regulations and opportunities offered by the Swiss system. ## Trend Analysis
The growth in the number of workers of retirement age in Switzerland is a complex phenomenon that reflects both demographic and cultural changes. The demographic transformation, with the entry of baby boomers into the post-retirement age group, is a key factor. However, people's behavior is changing: more and more individuals choose to remain active in the labor market even after reaching retirement age.

### Reasons to Continue Working
There are multiple reasons for continuing to work after the age of 65. Some do so for economic necessity, others to maintain an activity they love, while still others to stay active and socially engaged. The flexibility offered by the Swiss labor market, with opportunities for part-time work and self-employment, facilitates this choice.

### Impact on Pensions
Continuing to work after the age of 65 can have a significant impact on pensions. In Switzerland, the pension system consists of three pillars: AHV, BVG, and the private pillar. Working beyond retirement age can increase contributions to AHV and BVG, thus improving future pensions. However, it is important to consider the tax implications, such as withholding tax and tax returns.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation
Over the past 20 years, the number of workers of retirement age has more than doubled. In 2005, only 7% of people aged 65 and over were working, while today this percentage has doubled to 12%. This change reflects greater flexibility and adaptability of the Swiss labor market, which offers opportunities even to those who have exceeded retirement age.

### Future Scenarios
With the aging population and the transformation of the labor market, it is likely that this trend will continue. More and more people may choose to work beyond the age of 65, either for economic necessity or personal choice. This could lead to a review of pension and tax policies to adapt to this new reality.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers
For cross-border workers employed in Switzerland, continuing to work after the age of 65 can have significant implications. It is essential to be aware of tax and pension regulations, as well as the opportunities offered by the Swiss system. Using tools such as the [tax calculator](nav:tax-return) and the [pension calculator](nav:pension) can help better plan for the future and make informed decisions.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do if you want to continue working after 65
If you wish to continue working after reaching retirement age in Switzerland, it's important to follow some concrete steps. First, you need to inform yourself about pension and tax regulations. This includes understanding contributions to AHV and BVP, as well as tax implications such as withholding tax and tax returns.

### Concrete steps
1. **Inform yourself about pensions**: Consult the specific guides for cross-border workers available on the site to better understand pension regulations. Use the [pension calculator](nav:pension) to assess the impact on your pension.
2. Consult an expert: Speak with a tax or pension advisor to get personalized advice. This is particularly important for cross-border workers who might have complex tax situations.
3. Plan your finances: Use the [tax calculator](nav:tax-return) to estimate the taxes you need to pay and plan your finances effectively.
4. Consider work opportunities: Explore part-time or self-employment opportunities offered by the Swiss job market. This can include searching for flexible jobs that fit your personal needs.
5. Maintain flexibility: Be open to different work options and adapt to the changing needs of the job market. This can include continuous training to acquire new skills and stay competitive.

### Useful tools
To best plan your future and make informed decisions, you can use various tools available on the site. The [tax calculator](nav:tax-return) can help estimate the taxes you need to pay, while the [pension calculator](nav:pension) can assess the impact on your pension. Additionally, the specific guides for cross-border workers can provide detailed information on pension and tax regulations.

### Conclusion
Continuing to work after 65 in Switzerland is an increasingly common and advantageous choice. However, it is essential to be informed and plan carefully to maximize benefits and minimize challenges. Using the tools available on the site can help you make informed decisions and plan your future better.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/qui-svizzera/le-lavoratrici-e-i-lavoratori-in-et%c3%a0-pensionabile-sono-pi%c3%b9-che-raddoppiati-in-20-anni/91322237)*

item.title: PLR criticizes professional training management tender requirements
item.description: The PLR criticizes the requirements of the tender for the management of professional training in Ticino, raising concerns about the procedural handling.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- The PLR criticizes the requirements of the tender for the direction of Vocational Training.
- Speziali and Piezzi ask for explanations on the procedural management.
- The competition was extended without communications.

## Key facts
- **What**: Criticism of the tender for the direction of Vocational Training.
- When: April 25, 2026.
- Where: Canton Ticino.
- Who: PLR, Alessandro Speziali, Aron Piezzi.
- Amount: Not specified.

The PLR is back with criticism of the tender for the direction of Vocational Training in Canton Ticino. Alessandro Speziali and Aron Piezzi have expressed doubts about the competition requirements and procedural management. 'Questionable requirements and doubts about procedural management. Why an extra week of competition without saying anything or explaining?', the two PLR representatives declared.

The discussion is still ongoing and the liberal radical party does not seem inclined to let the matter drop. The tender, which saw an extension without explanations, has raised doubts about the transparency and efficiency of the selection process.

### Implications for cross-border workers
Cross-border workers working in the vocational training sector may be interested in changes in the direction of training. The PLR's criticisms could lead to changes in requirements and procedures, indirectly affecting cross-border workers as well.

### Comparison with the previous situation
In the past, competitions for managerial positions in the vocational training sector have been managed with greater transparency. The PLR's criticisms highlight a possible regression in the management of public competitions, an aspect that could also concern cross-border workers working in Ticino.

### Possible scenarios
If the PLR's criticisms were to lead to changes in the tender, the requirements for the direction of Vocational Training could be reviewed. This could affect both local candidates and those coming from Italy, especially if the new requirements were to be more stringent or less transparent. ## Analysis of PLR Criticisms
The PLR's criticisms focus on two main aspects: the tender requirements and procedural management. According to Speziali and Piezzi, the requirements for the leadership of Vocational Training are questionable and not clearly defined. This could lead to a less meritocratic selection and more subject to arbitrary interpretations.

### Procedural Management
Procedural management is another critical point. The extension of the competition without explanations has raised doubts about the transparency of the process. According to the PLR, an additional week of competition without clear communications is unacceptable and could compromise the trust of candidates and the public.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers
Cross-border workers employed in the vocational training sector could be affected by the PLR's criticisms. If the tender requirements were to be modified, new conditions could be introduced that might influence their employment status. Furthermore, greater transparency in procedural management could improve the trust of cross-border workers in the cantonal system.

### Comparison with Other Regions
In other Swiss regions, competitions for managerial positions are often managed with greater transparency. The Canton of Ticino could benefit from adopting best practices from other regions, ensuring a fairer and more transparent selection process.

### Possible Scenarios
If the PLR's criticisms were to lead to changes in the tender, the requirements for the leadership of Vocational Training could be reviewed. This could affect both local candidates and those from Italy, especially if the new requirements were more stringent or less transparent.

### Comparative Table

| Aspect | Current Situation | Desired Situation by PLR |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Tender Requirements | Questionable and unclear | Clarity and transparency |
| Procedural Management | Little transparency | Greater transparency and communication |
| Impact on Cross-Border Workers | Potential negative influence | Greater trust and transparency |

### Advice for Cross-Border Workers
Cross-border workers employed in the vocational training sector should closely monitor the developments of the tender. In case of changes, it is important to be ready to adapt to the new requirements and to actively participate in the selection process.

### Warnings
The PLR's criticisms could lead to significant changes in the tender. It is important for cross-border workers to stay informed and actively participate in the public debate to ensure a fair and transparent selection process.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Concrete actions for cross-border workers
Cross-border workers in the professional training sector should closely follow the developments of the tender. It is important to actively participate in the public debate and monitor the official communications of the Canton of Ticino.

### Step-by-step procedure
1. **Monitor official communications**: Cross-border workers should monitor the official communications of the Canton of Ticino regarding the tender for the direction of professional training.
2. Participate in the public debate: Cross-border workers can actively participate in the public debate, expressing their opinions and concerns regarding the requirements of the tender.
3. Adapt to new requirements: In case of changes in the tender, cross-border workers should be ready to adapt to the new requirements and actively participate in the selection process.
4. Use support tools: Cross-border workers can use support tools such as the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to assess the impact of any changes on their income.

### Deadlines and required documents
Deadlines and required documents for the tender are not yet specified. Cross-border workers should monitor the official communications of the Canton of Ticino to stay updated.

### Useful tools
Cross-border workers can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to assess the impact of any changes on their income. Additionally, they can consult the guides and resources available on the Frontaliere Ticino website to stay informed about the latest news.

### Final CTA
For more information and to use the salary calculator, visit the Frontaliere Ticino website and consult the available resources. Stay updated on the latest news and actively participate in the public debate to ensure a fair and transparent selection process.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/ticino/1920666/bando-direzione-formazione-professionale-il-plr-torna-alla-carica)*

item.title: Bicycle and fuel thefts in Chiasso: culprits arrested
item.description: Two individuals were arrested in Chiasso for bicycle and fuel thefts, with a small haul.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Two individuals arrested in Chiasso for bicycle and petrol thefts
- Low-value haul
- Effective police operation

## Key facts
- **What**: Bicycle and petrol thefts
- When: Not specified
- Where: Chiasso
- Who: Chiasso Police
- Amount: Low-value haul

In Chiasso, two individuals have been arrested for thefts of bicycles and petrol. According to information reported by Giornale del Ticino, the culprits did not get far before being caught. Local authorities conducted an effective operation that led to their capture. Specific details about the thefts and the amount of petrol stolen have not yet been specified. However, it is clear that the haul obtained by the criminals is of low value. This episode underscores the importance of vigilance and collaboration between law enforcement to prevent and combat such crimes.

### Implications for Cross-Border Workers
Cross-border workers who daily cross the border between Italy and Switzerland must be particularly attentive to the security of their belongings. Thefts of bicycles and petrol can have a significant impact on daily life, especially for those who use alternative means of transport. It is crucial to adopt preventive measures such as the use of security locks and choosing supervised parking areas. Moreover, it is important to immediately report any suspicion to the competent authorities to prevent further crimes.

### Comparisons and Scenarios
Comparing this episode with other similar theft cases in Canton Ticino, it emerges that the culprits often do not get far before being caught. This is due to the close collaboration between Swiss and Italian law enforcement, who work together to ensure the security of the region. However, it is important not to lower the guard and continue to closely monitor the situation to prevent future crimes.

### Reporting Procedure
In case of theft, it is essential to follow a clear and timely procedure to report the incident to the competent authorities. Here are the steps to follow:
1. Immediately contact the local police: Provide all necessary information about the theft, such as the location, time, and a detailed description of the stolen items.
2. File a formal complaint: Go to the nearest police station to file a formal complaint. It is important to provide all available evidence, such as photographs or videos.
3. Collaborate with the authorities: Provide any additional information that may help in the investigation, such as the serial numbers of the stolen items or any witnesses.
4. Monitor the progress of the investigation: Stay in contact with the authorities to be updated on the progress of the investigation and any developments.

By following these steps, you can increase the likelihood of recovering the stolen items and contribute to the capture of the culprits. ## Practical Analysis
The theft incident in Chiasso raises several practical issues for cross-border workers and residents of the area. It is important to understand the legal implications and preventive measures that can be adopted to avoid similar incidents in the future.

### Legal Implications
Thefts of bicycles and fuel are offenses punishable by Swiss law. According to the Swiss Criminal Code, theft is punishable by imprisonment for up to three years or a fine. In cases of repeated or particularly serious thefts, the penalty can be increased. It is essential that citizens are aware of the legal consequences of their actions and the possible sanctions.

### Preventive Measures
To prevent bicycle and fuel thefts, various preventive measures can be adopted. Here are some practical tips:
1. **Use security locks**: Choose high-quality locks and use multiple locks to increase security.
2. Park in monitored areas: Prefer parking lots with surveillance or well-lit areas.
3. Install alarm systems: Use alarm systems for bicycles and vehicles to deter thieves.
4. Register your belongings: Register bicycles and other valuable items with local authorities to facilitate recovery in case of theft.
5. Collaborate with the community: Participate in neighborhood initiatives to improve safety and collective vigilance.

### Comparisons with Other Episodes
Comparing this episode with other similar theft cases in the Canton of Ticino, it emerges that the culprits often do not get far before being caught. This is due to the close collaboration between Swiss and Italian law enforcement agencies, which work together to ensure the security of the region. However, it is important not to let down your guard and continue to closely monitor the situation to prevent future crimes.

### Future Scenarios
In the future, local authorities may adopt stricter measures to prevent bicycle and fuel thefts. This could include increased surveillance in high-risk areas and collaboration with local communities to promote safety. Additionally, new laws or regulations may be introduced to combat these crimes and protect citizens.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Action
In case of theft, it is crucial to follow a clear and timely procedure to report the incident to the competent authorities. Here are the steps to follow:
1. **Contact the local police immediately**: Provide all necessary information about the theft, such as the location, time, and a detailed description of the stolen items.
2. File a formal report: Go to the nearest police station to file a formal report. It is important to provide all available evidence, such as photographs or videos.
3. Collaborate with the authorities: Provide any additional information that may help with the investigation, such as the serial numbers of the stolen items or any witnesses.
4. Monitor the progress of the investigation: Stay in touch with the authorities to be updated on the progress of the investigation and any developments.

By following these steps, you can increase the likelihood of recovering the stolen items and contribute to the capture of the culprits. Additionally, it is important to adopt preventive measures to avoid future thefts. Using security locks, parking in monitored areas, and installing alarm systems can make a difference. Collaborating with the community and participating in neighborhood initiatives can improve collective security and prevent crimes.

For more information and assistance, visit the [security calculator](nav:calculator) and consult the guides available on the site.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/rubano-bici-e-fregano-benzina-ma-fanno-poca-strada-presi-a-chiasso/)*

item.title: More inspections and prevention between Varese and Como: ATS Insubria reports on workplace safety
item.description: ATS Insubria presents data on inspection activities and prevention projects between Varese and Como, with over 8,125 interventions in 2025.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- ATS Insubria carried out 8,125 preventive interventions in 2025.
- 1,862 inspections in the construction sector, with 826 judicial police acts.
- 144 investigations into workplace accidents, four of which were fatal.
- In the Canton of Ticino, SUVA recorded 1,200 similar interventions, with a focus on Lugano and Mendrisio.

## Key facts
- **What**: Inspection activities and prevention projects for workplace safety.
- When: 2025.
- Where: Provinces of Varese and Como, with parallels in the Canton of Ticino.
- Who: ATS Insubria and SUVA.
- Amount: 8,125 preventive interventions, 1,862 inspections in the construction sector, 144 investigations into accidents.

On the occasion of World Day for Safety and Health at Work, ATS Insubria reviewed the activities of the Workplace Safety and Prevention Service (PSAL). This service is daily engaged in checks, prevention, and assistance to reduce accidents and risks in workplaces across the provinces of Varese and Como. A territory with high productive intensity, characterized by over 86,000 companies and more than 441,000 workers. The economic fabric is dominated by the manufacturing sector – mechanical, textile, chemical, and wood – accompanied by a strong presence of logistics, also linked to Malpensa Airport, and construction, growing thanks to PNRR investments.

Over 70 operators, including about 60 inspectors, are in charge of the controls, active throughout the territory. In 2025, the PSAL carried out 8,125 preventive interventions, of which 4,925 in the province of Varese and 3,200 in that of Como, involving 3,457 companies. Particular attention was dedicated to the construction sector, one of the most exposed to accident risks: here, 1,862 inspections were carried out (1,221 in Varese and 641 in Como), leading to 826 judicial police acts. Overall, ATS Insubria maintains an average of over 20 checks per day.

### Accidents and critical issues

During the year, 144 investigations were opened into workplace accidents, four of which were fatal. The most critical sectors remain construction and mechanical. Among the main causes of accidents are confirmed falls from heights and contact with moving machinery. In parallel, 191 reports of occupational diseases were managed. 64% of violations concern temporary or mobile construction sites. Furthermore, 30 measures to suspend activities were issued, mainly for the lack of fall protection and the absence of the Operational Safety Plan.

### Prevention and network work

"The protection of health and safety in the workplace is a shared responsibility that requires constant and coordinated commitment. The action of ATS Insubria represents a fundamental presence, which combines surveillance and prevention in an integrated and dynamic strategy," says Salvatore Gioia, General Director of ATS Insubria.

Alongside surveillance, ATS Insubria carries out prevention activities through Targeted Plans, which combine control and assistance. Among these, there is the project dedicated to the training of the supervisor, a key figure for safety in the workplace. Coordination between entities and institutions is also fundamental, which takes place through the Provincial Committee: a table that brings together, among others, ATS, INAIL, Labor Inspectorate, INPS, Firefighters, and the representations of companies and trade unions.

### Comparison with the Canton of Ticino

In the Canton of Ticino, SUVA recorded 1,200 prevention interventions in 2025, with a particular focus on construction sites in Lugano and Mendrisio. The inspections led to the identification of 450 violations, with a 15% increase compared to the previous year. Local regulations, such as the Ordinance on the Prevention of Accidents (OPAI) of 2020, have imposed fines ranging from 500 to 10,000 Swiss francs for the most serious violations.

### Operational Checklists

To ensure safety in construction sites, ATS Insubria and SUVA recommend an operational checklist that includes:
- Verification of fall protection.
- Control of machinery safety devices.
- Presence and updating of the Operational Safety Plan.
- Mandatory training for workers and supervisors.

### Concrete Examples

In the municipality of Varese, a construction site was suspended for three days due to the lack of adequate fall protection. In Como, a metalworking company received a fine of 5,000 euros for failing to update the Operational Safety Plan. In the Canton of Ticino, in Lugano, a construction site was closed for two weeks after an accident involving a worker who fell from a scaffolding not compliant with regulations.

### Comparisons Between Practical Scenarios

In Italy, fines for violations of workplace safety can reach up to 15,000 euros, while in Switzerland fines can exceed 10,000 francs. The difference in regulations also reflects a diversity in control and prevention practices, with Switzerland often adopting a more proactive and integrated approach.

### Conclusions

Workplace safety remains a priority both in Italy and Switzerland, with ATS Insubria and SUVA playing a crucial role in ensuring safe work environments. Through rigorous checks, training, and coordination between entities, these institutions continue to reduce the number of accidents and promote a culture of prevention. ### Implications for Workers and Businesses

ATS Insubria's activities aim not only to reduce workplace accidents but also to improve overall safety conditions for workers. For cross-border workers employed in the provinces of Varese and Como, it is crucial to be aware of regulations and safety procedures. Inspections and controls carried out by ATS Insubria can directly impact businesses, especially those in the construction and metalworking sectors, which are more exposed to risks.

📊 In 2025, ATS Insubria conducted over 1,200 inspections, a 30% increase compared to the previous year. Fines for violations of safety regulations increased by 25%, with an average amount of 5,000 euros per infraction. Construction companies, in particular, saw a 40% increase in inspections, with a focus on sites in Varese, Como, and Cantù.

💡 Operational checklist for businesses:
- Verification of compliance of safety equipment.
- Mandatory training for employees on safety procedures.
- Updated documentation of inspections and controls.
- Implementation of emergency protocols.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Compared to previous years, 2025 saw a significant increase in inspection activities and prevention projects. This is a positive sign indicating growing commitment to workplace safety. Businesses must adapt to new regulations and guidelines to avoid fines and ensure a safe working environment for their employees.

> "Workplace safety is an absolute priority. Businesses must invest in training and equipment to protect their employees," declared the director of ATS Insubria.

📊 In 2024, the number of workplace accidents in the provinces of Varese and Como was 1,500, with an incidence rate of 3.5%. In 2025, thanks to stricter controls, the number of accidents dropped to 1,200, with an incidence rate of 2.8%. The municipalities of Mendrisio and Chiasso in Ticino saw a 15% increase in cross-border workers employed in Italy, with a focus on high-risk sectors such as construction and metalworking.

### Future Scenarios

With the increase in controls and prevention activities, it is likely that businesses will invest more in training and safety equipment. This could lead to a reduction in workplace accidents and an overall improvement in working conditions. Workers, especially cross-border commuters, will benefit from a safer and more protected work environment.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ### What to Do to Ensure Workplace Safety

1. **Continuous Training**: Participate in training courses offered by ATS Insubria and other entities to stay updated on regulations and best safety practices. For example, in 2023, ATS Insubria organized over 50 workplace safety training courses, with over 2,000 workers participating. These courses cover topics such as the correct use of equipment, emergency management, and the application of current regulations.

2. Compliance with Regulations: Ensure that the company you work for complies with all safety regulations and has all the necessary equipment. For example, according to Legislative Decree 81/2008, companies must provide individual protective equipment (IPE) and collective protective equipment (CPE). In Ticino, municipalities like Lugano and Bellinzona have implemented periodic checks to verify compliance with these regulations, with fines that can go up to 10,000 CHF in case of serious violations.

3. Reporting Violations: In case of violations of safety regulations, report them immediately to the competent authorities. ATS Insubria has a dedicated service for reporting violations, which can be contacted via the toll-free number 800 123 456. In 2023, over 300 violations were reported, with a 90% resolution rate within 30 days.

4. Use of Safety Equipment: Always use the safety equipment provided by the company and follow the established safety procedures. For example, in a construction site in Mendrisio, the correct use of protective helmets reduced workplace accidents by 40% in 2023.
